engineGuide.h2: Mercedes Benz CLS Engine Codes - Full Technical Specifications & Replacement Guide
engineGuide.h3: Every engine code fitted to the Mercedes Benz CLS across three generations (C219, C218, C257), with technical specs, compatible variants and trims, known failure points, and average rebuilt prices from UK specialists. Select any engine code to compare quotes.

entries.title: 3.0L Diesel
entries.history: The OM642 3.0-litre V6 diesel powered the vast majority of UK CLS models from 2006 to 2018, dominating fleet and high-mileage private ownership with strong torque delivery .
compatibleVariants: CLS 320 CDI, CLS 350 CDI, CLS 350 CDI Sport, CLS 350 CDI AMG Sport
commonFailures: Oil cooler seal leakage leading to coolant contamination and oil loss
Turbocharger oil seal failure causing excessive oil consumption
DPF and swirl flap issues on higher-mileage units
entries.cta: Get quotes for OM642.920 Mercedes Benz CLS engine replacement

entries.title: 3.0L Diesel (BiTurbo)
entries.history: The twin-turbo version of the OM642 was reserved for the CLS 400 CDI, delivering 265PS and significantly sharper throttle response for performance-focused diesel buyers.
compatibleVariants: CLS 400 CDI, CLS 400 CDI AMG Sport
commonFailures: Oil cooler seal failure (shared with single-turbo OM642)
Turbo actuator failure leading to power loss and limp mode
EGR cooler blockage causing reduced performance
entries.cta: Get quotes for OM642.940 Mercedes Benz CLS engine replacement

entries.title: 2.1L Diesel
entries.history: Mercedes’ 2.1-litre four-cylinder diesel brought efficiency and lower running costs to the CLS range from 2011, making the CLS 250 CDI an accessible entry point to the four-door coupe segment .
compatibleVariants: CLS 250 CDI, CLS 250 CDI AMG Sport, CLS 220 BlueTEC
commonFailures: EGR valve failure causing rough idle and check engine light
Swirl flap mechanism failure in intake manifold reducing power
AdBlue system faults (P13DF heater circuit / P20E8 low pressure)
entries.cta: Get quotes for OM651.924 Mercedes Benz CLS engine replacement

entries.title: 2.0L Diesel
entries.history: The OM654 replaced the OM651 in the C257 generation CLS, featuring a full-aluminium construction, stepped combustion bowls, and Euro 6d emissions compliance for cleaner, more efficient operation.
compatibleVariants: CLS 300 d, CLS 300 d AMG Line
commonFailures: AdBlue system faults requiring module recalibration
Timing chain stretch in higher-mileage units
Injector seal leaks causing diesel odour and engine noise
entries.cta: Get quotes for OM654.920 Mercedes Benz CLS engine replacement

entries.title: 3.5L Petrol
entries.history: The M272 3.5-litre V6 powered first-generation CLS 350 models from 2005, offering a smooth, high-revving alternative to the diesel range. It remains a popular engine for classic CLS enthusiasts.
compatibleVariants: CLS 350, CLS 350 Sport, CLS 350 AMG Sport
commonFailures: Timing chain tensioner failure causing rattling noise on startup
Balance shaft sprocket wear (earlier pre-2008 models)
Intake manifold runner flap failure
entries.cta: Get quotes for M272.964 Mercedes Benz CLS engine replacement

entries.title: 3.5L Petrol
entries.history: The M276 replaced the M272 from 2011 with direct injection, a lighter all-aluminium construction, and improved thermal efficiency. It powered both CLS 350 and CLS 400 models through the C218 generation .
compatibleVariants: CLS 350, CLS 400, CLS 350 Sport, CLS 400 AMG Sport
commonFailures: Carbon buildup on direct injection intake valves requiring cleaning
Timing chain stretch at higher mileages
Camshaft adjuster solenoid failures
entries.cta: Get quotes for M276.820 Mercedes Benz CLS engine replacement

entries.title: 4.7L Petrol Biturbo
entries.history: The M278 4.7-litre twin-turbo V8 delivered effortless performance for CLS 500 models, combining Mercedes’ third-generation direct injection with piezo injectors and a spray-guided combustion process.
commonFailures: Turbocharger actuator failure causing boost pressure loss
High-pressure fuel pump wear
Timing chain tensioner issues on higher-mileage units
entries.cta: Get quotes for M278.920 Mercedes Benz CLS engine replacement

entries.title: 6.2L Petrol
entries.history: The M156 6.2-litre V8 was Mercedes-AMG’s first independently developed engine. It powered the CLS 63 AMG with a naturally aspirated 6.2-litre screamer that defined high-performance luxury for its generation .
compatibleVariants: CLS 63 AMG, CLS 63 AMG Performance Package
commonFailures: Head bolt failure leading to coolant in cylinders - a well-documented specification issue affecting early M156 engines
Camshaft adjuster wear causing rattle and performance loss
Hydraulic tappet noise on higher-mileage units
entries.cta: Get quotes for M156.983 Mercedes Benz CLS engine replacement

entries.title: 5.5L Petrol Biturbo
entries.history: The M157 5.5-litre twin-turbo V8 replaced the naturally aspirated M156 for the C218 CLS 63 AMG, delivering substantially more torque and effortless mid-range power while maintaining AMG’s performance DNA.
compatibleVariants: CLS 63 AMG, CLS 63 AMG S, CLS 63 AMG S 4MATIC, CLS 63 AMG Shooting Brake
commonFailures: High-pressure fuel pump failure causing starting issues
Turbocharger oil feed line blockages
Camshaft adjuster solenoid wear
entries.cta: Get quotes for M157.981 Mercedes Benz CLS engine replacement

entries.title: 3.0L Mild Hybrid (EQ Boost)
entries.history: The M256 3.0-litre inline-six introduced mild hybrid technology to the CLS for the C257 generation, featuring an integrated starter-alternator (EQ Boost) and electric auxiliary compressor for instant throttle response and reduced emissions .
compatibleVariants: CLS 450, CLS 450 4MATIC, CLS 53 AMG, CLS 53 AMG 4MATIC+
commonFailures: 48V electrical system faults affecting EQ Boost functionality
Turbocharger failure - reported on M256 engines, particularly with increased power outputs
Coolant pump leaks
entries.cta: Get quotes for M256.930 Mercedes Benz CLS engine replacement

commonProblems.h2: Mercedes Benz CLS Engine Problems - What It Costs to Fix & When Replacement Makes Sense

problems.group: Mercedes Benz CLS OM642 Oil Cooler Seal Failure
problems.h4: Mercedes Benz CLS OM642 Oil Cooler Seal Failure - Why V6 Diesel Engines Leak Coolant Into Oil
problems.rootCause: The rubber oil cooler seals harden and crack under repeated heat cycles, allowing engine oil and coolant to mix. This creates a mayonnaise-like emulsion, blocks oil passages, and progressively starves the turbo and bearings of clean oil.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Replace oil cooler seals, flush cooling system, change oil and filter
repairOptions.longevity: 60,000+ miles when done before contamination damages bearings

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Replace oil cooler seals plus turbocharger if oil starvation has damaged bearings
repairOptions.longevity: Restores full performance; viable if bottom end is still healthy

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Supply and fit fully rebuilt OM642 with upgraded Viton seals, new oil cooler, timing components, and bench testing
repairOptions.longevity: Factory-spec reliability with upgraded components; 12-month unlimited warranty

problems.recommendation: If caught early (no coolant in oil, no low oil pressure warnings), the preventative seal replacement is excellent value and should be done immediately. Once you see emulsified oil on the dipstick or under the oil cap, the turbo is often already damaged. On a CLS worth £4,000-£8,000, full replacement makes sense if the vehicle is otherwise well-maintained; specialist rebuilds with upgraded seals are considerably cheaper than dealer replacement engines.
problems.cta: Compare rebuilt OM642 engine prices for your Mercedes Benz CLS

problems.group: Mercedes Benz CLS M272 Timing Chain & Balance Shaft Failure
problems.h4: Mercedes Benz CLS M272 Timing Chain & Balance Shaft Failure - The V6 Petrol Rattle
problems.rootCause: The M272’s timing chain tensioner loses hydraulic pressure, allowing the chain to slap against guides. Early pre-2008 engines also suffer from a poorly hardened balance shaft sprocket that wears prematurely, throwing off cam timing and often triggering check engine lights.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Replace timing chain, tensioner, and upper guides without removing engine
repairOptions.longevity: 60,000+ miles; only suitable if balance shaft sprocket is not worn

repairOptions.tier: Full - Timing Set & Balance Shaft Repair
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Replace chain, tensioner, guides, balance shaft sprocket, and camshaft adjusters
repairOptions.longevity: Full engine timing restoration; appropriate for engines with fault codes P0016/P0017

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Supply and fit rebuilt M272 with upgraded timing components and post-2008 spec parts
repairOptions.longevity: Complete peace of mind with upgraded timing components; 12-month warranty

problems.recommendation: If you hear a brief rattle on cold start but no check engine light, the tensioner replacement alone may buy you years of service. Once fault codes for cam/crank correlation appear, the balance shaft sprocket has worn - at that point, a full timing set replacement or rebuilt engine is the only reliable fix. On a C219 CLS 350 worth £3,000-£5,000, weigh repair costs carefully against the car’s overall condition.
problems.cta: Get quotes for M272 rebuilt engine for your Mercedes Benz CLS 350

problems.group: Mercedes Benz CLS M156 Head Bolt Failure
problems.h4: Mercedes Benz CLS M156 Head Bolt Failure - The Early 6.2L AMG Catastrophe
problems.typicalFailureMileage: 30,000 - 60,000 miles (varies widely; some fail early, others never)
problems.rootCause: The original M156 head bolts had an insufficiently long threaded shank and a problematic torque-to-yield design. Over time, they stretch or snap, allowing coolant to seep into cylinders. Cold start misfires, white smoke, and slow coolant loss are the classic warning signs.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Replace all head bolts with updated ARP or factory-spec longer bolts; no head gasket failure yet
repairOptions.longevity: Permanent fix; only viable if coolant has NOT entered cylinders and no overheating has occurred

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Replace head gaskets, bolts, and machine heads if warped; flush cooling system
repairOptions.longevity: Restores engine integrity; required if coolant has contaminated cylinders

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Supply and fit fully rebuilt M156 with upgraded head bolts, new timing components, and AMG-spec bench testing
repairOptions.longevity: Complete peace of mind with all known weaknesses addressed; 12-month unlimited warranty

problems.recommendation: If your CLS 63 AMG has no symptoms, a preventative head bolt upgrade at an AMG specialist is the single best insurance policy you can buy. Once you have misfires or white smoke, the damage is done - replacement or a full rebuild with head machining is the only path. On a car worth £15,000-£25,000, a rebuilt engine with upgraded bolts is often the smarter long-term investment than chasing band-aid repairs.
problems.cta: Compare M156 rebuilt AMG engine prices for your CLS 63 AMG

problems.group: Mercedes Benz CLS OM651 Injector & Swirl Flap Failure
problems.h4: Mercedes Benz CLS OM651 Injector & Swirl Flap Failure - Four-Cylinder Diesel Woes
problems.affectedModels: Mercedes Benz CLS 250 CDI, CLS 220 BlueTEC (C218 2011-2017, OM651.924)
problems.rootCause: Piezo injector seals leak compression gases past the injector, causing a characteristic ticking or chuffing noise and carbon buildup. Simultaneously, the plastic swirl flaps in the intake manifold become brittle and break, with fragments sometimes entering cylinders.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Remove injectors, clean bores, replace seals and copper washers
repairOptions.longevity: 40,000+ miles; permanent fix for seal-related ticking noises

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Replace complete intake manifold with swirl flap assembly
repairOptions.longevity: Restores power and prevents flap fragments entering engine

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Supply and fit rebuilt OM651 with upgraded injector seals, new intake manifold, and timing chain
repairOptions.longevity: Complete drivetrain restoration; required if swirl flap fragments have damaged cylinders

problems.recommendation: Seal and swirl flap repairs are routine on higher-mileage OM651 engines. Catch them early and the CLS 250 CDI remains a very economical car to run. If you ignore the ticking noise and power loss, broken flap fragments can score cylinder walls - at that point, only a replacement engine makes sense. Specialist pricing is dramatically lower than dealer for these jobs.
problems.cta: Get OM651 rebuilt engine quotes for your Mercedes Benz CLS 250 CDI

problems.group: Mercedes Benz CLS M157 High-Pressure Fuel Pump Failure
problems.h4: Mercedes Benz CLS M157 High-Pressure Fuel Pump Failure - 5.5L Biturbo AMG Starvation
problems.affectedModels: Mercedes Benz CLS 63 AMG, CLS 63 AMG S (C218 2011-2017, M157.981)
problems.rootCause: The M157’s high-pressure fuel pump (HPFP) suffers from internal wear, often due to fuel contamination or inadequate lubrication. As the pump fails, rail pressure drops under load, causing hesitation, misfires, and eventual crank-no-start conditions.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Replace high-pressure fuel pump, flush fuel system, replace fuel filter
repairOptions.longevity: Restores full fuel pressure; permanent fix if injectors are still healthy

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Replace HPFP, all six injectors, and fuel rail pressure sensor
repairOptions.longevity: Complete fuel system renewal; required if pump debris has damaged injectors

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Supply and fit rebuilt M157 with upgraded HPFP, new injectors, and full AMG-spec bench test
repairOptions.longevity: Only justified if bottom end damage has occurred from fuel dilution or neglect

problems.recommendation: HPFP failure on the M157 is serious but rarely requires a full engine unless ignored. Replace the pump at the first sign of prolonged cranking or hesitation. If you continue driving, fuel dilution can wipe out bearings. On a CLS 63 AMG worth £20,000-£35,000, a specialist HPFP and injector replacement is strong value and should be paired with a fuel system flush.
problems.cta: Compare M157 rebuilt AMG engine prices for your Mercedes Benz CLS 63 AMG

problems.group: Mercedes Benz CLS M256 48V EQ Boost Electrical Failure
problems.h4: Mercedes Benz CLS M256 48V EQ Boost Electrical Failure - Mild Hybrid Complexity
problems.affectedModels: Mercedes Benz CLS 450, CLS 53 AMG (C257 2018-present, M256.930)
problems.rootCause: The 48V electrical system that powers the integrated starter-alternator (EQ Boost) and electric auxiliary compressor can suffer from battery module degradation, software corruption, or water ingress in the 48V connector.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Dealer-level software recalibration of 48V control modules
repairOptions.longevity: May resolve intermittent faults; only suitable for warning lights without driveability issues

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Replace 48V lithium-ion battery module and recalibrate system
repairOptions.longevity: Restores EQ Boost and electric compressor function

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Supply and fit rebuilt M256 with updated 48V components and full software configuration
repairOptions.longevity: Only required if internal engine damage has occurred from hybrid system faults; rare

problems.recommendation: M256 electrical faults are frustrating but rarely write off the engine itself. Start with a specialist diagnostic and software update before replacing hardware. Full engine replacement is exceptionally rare on this engine family - most issues stop at the 48V battery or software level. Use a Mercedes-Benz specialist with M256 hybrid experience, not a general independent.
problems.cta: Get quotes for M256 rebuilt engine for your Mercedes Benz CLS 450 / 53 AMG

finalCta.h4: Don't Let Engine Failure Write Off Your Mercedes Benz CLS
finalCta.paragraph: Engine replacement doesn't have to mean the end of your CLS. Whether you're dealing with an OM642 oil leak, an M272 timing rattle, or an AMG head bolt failure, EngineMarket makes it simple to compare prices from trusted UK Mercedes Benz CLS specialists. Every rebuilt engine in our network is bench-tested, fitted with upgraded components to address the original failure point, and backed by a minimum 12-month unlimited mileage warranty. Compare prices, choose your preferred specialist, and get back on the road without the main dealer premium.
finalCta.buttonText: Compare Mercedes Benz CLS Engine Replacement Prices Now →
finalCta.disclaimer: All prices are indicative UK market ranges based on historical quote data and repair requests from our network of engine specialists. Dealer pricing is estimated based on published main dealer labour rates and genuine OEM parts pricing. Specialist pricing reflects actual quote data from EngineMarket’s UK network. Actual costs vary depending on damage severity, vehicle condition, variant, and workshop location within the UK. Use our free comparison tool to get tailored quotes for your specific Mercedes Benz CLS and engine code. Every engine supplied through EngineMarket carries a minimum 12‑month unlimited mileage warranty - always confirm exact terms with your chosen supplier.

engineSizes.h2: Mercedes Benz CLS Engines by Size - Find the Right Displacement for Your Variant
engineSizes.intro: Every displacement fitted to the Mercedes Benz CLS across three generations is listed below, with compatible engine codes, production years and common failure notes.

groups.title: Mercedes Benz CLS Diesel Engine Sizes

items.title: Mercedes Benz CLS 2.1L Diesel Engines
items.description: The 2.1-litre four-cylinder diesel, coded OM651, joined the CLS range with the second-generation C218 model in 2011 as the entry-level diesel option. Badged as the CLS 250 CDI (and later CLS 220 BlueTEC), this engine prioritised fuel economy and lower CO2 emissions over outright performance, making the CLS accessible to a broader audience.
commonFailurePoints: swirl flap mechanism failure in intake manifold
items.cta: Get quotes for Mercedes Benz CLS 2.1L diesel engines

items.title: Mercedes Benz CLS 3.0L Diesel Engines
items.description: The 3.0-litre V6 diesel, part of Mercedes' OM642 family, was the backbone of the CLS diesel range from the first generation C219 through to the late C218 models. Initially offered as the CLS 320 CDI, it was later branded as the CLS 350 CDI and CLS 350 BlueTEC. A higher-output twin-turbo version powered the CLS 400 CDI, delivering 265hp.
commonFailurePoints: Oil cooler seal leakage causing coolant-oil mixing
items.cta: Get quotes for Mercedes Benz CLS 3.0L diesel engines

groups.title: Mercedes Benz CLS Petrol Engine Sizes

items.title: Mercedes Benz CLS 3.5L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 3.5-litre V6 petrol engine powered the original CLS 350 from the C219 launch in 2004, later receiving an update to the M276 direct-injection unit in the C218 generation. This engine represented the smooth, refined petrol alternative to the diesel-heavy CLS line-up.
items.cta: Compare Mercedes Benz CLS 3.5L petrol engine prices

items.title: Mercedes Benz CLS 4.7L Petrol Engines
items.description: The M278 4.7-litre twin-turbo V8 replaced the earlier naturally aspirated 5.0 and 5.5 V8s in the second-generation C218 CLS. Badged as the CLS 500, this engine delivered effortless torque and genuine luxury performance, combining twin turbos with spray-guided direct injection.
commonFailurePoints: Turbo actuator failure causing boost pressure loss
items.cta: Compare Mercedes Benz CLS 4.7L petrol engine prices

items.title: Mercedes Benz CLS 5.5L Petrol Engines
items.description: The M157 5.5-litre twin-turbo V8 powered the C218 CLS 63 AMG, replacing the naturally aspirated M156 from the first generation. This engine became the heart of the performance CLS, delivering immense torque and effortless power delivery that defined the AMG experience of its era.
items.cta: Compare Mercedes Benz CLS 5.5L petrol engine prices

items.title: Mercedes Benz CLS 6.2L Petrol Engines
items.description: The M156 6.2-litre naturally aspirated V8 was Mercedes-AMG's first independently developed engine, and it powered the first-generation C219 CLS 63 AMG to great acclaim. This high-revving, emotionally charged engine set the template for AMG's V8 dominance and remains a favourite among enthusiasts despite its known weaknesses.
commonFailurePoints: Head bolt failure leading to coolant in cylinders
items.cta: Compare Mercedes Benz CLS 6.2L petrol engine prices

groups.title: Mercedes Benz CLS MHEV Engine Sizes

items.title: Mercedes Benz CLS 2.0L MHEV Petrol Engines
items.description: The M264 2.0-litre four-cylinder mild hybrid engine arrived with the third-generation C257 CLS in 2018, replacing larger naturally aspirated units with a smaller turbocharged engine supplemented by a 48V EQ Boost starter-alternator. Badged as the CLS 350, this represented Mercedes' shift toward electrification while maintaining reasonable performance.
items.cta: Enquire about Mercedes Benz CLS 2.0L MHEV engine availability

items.title: Mercedes Benz CLS 3.0L MHEV Petrol Engines
items.description: The M256 3.0-litre inline-six mild hybrid engine represented a return to the straight-six configuration for Mercedes, featuring an integrated EQ Boost starter-alternator and an electric auxiliary compressor for immediate throttle response. This engine powered both the CLS 450 and the performance-oriented CLS 53 AMG in the C257 generation, blending efficiency with genuine sporting character. Not sure which engine size your Mercedes Benz CLS needs? Enter your registration number above - we'll identify the exact displacement, engine code and compatible replacement options instantly. All engines supplied through EngineMarket include a minimum 12-month unlimited mileage warranty from UK Mercedes Benz CLS specialists.
commonFailurePoints: 48V electrical system faults affecting EQ Boost
items.cta: Enquire about Mercedes Benz CLS 3.0L MHEV engine availability

engineYears.h2: Mercedes Benz CLS Engines by Year - What Was Fitted & When
engineYears.intro: This timeline covers every major engine change across the CLS's three generations - from the pioneering C219 (2004-2010) to the current C257 (2018-present). Use the year blocks below to identify your exact engine and compare rebuilt prices from UK specialists.

CLS 350 (3.5L V6 M272) joins the range
CLS 320 CDI (3.0L V6 diesel OM642) arrives 2005
SBC (Sensotronic Brake Control) system fitted on early cars
mainEngines: M113 E55 AMG - 5.4L supercharged V8 (CLS 55 AMG, 469hp)
OM642 DE30 - 3.0L V6 turbo diesel (CLS 320 CDI, 221hp)
knownFor: SBC brake system can be costly to repair when it fails
M272 V6 timing chain tensioner weakness becomes apparent later
CLS 55 AMG supercharged V8 offers strong tuning potential
C219 interior is strictly four-seat layout with no rear bench

CLS 500 upgraded to 5.5L M273 V8 (382hp, rebadged CLS 550)
CLS 350 CDI diesel variant added with higher torque output
CLS 280 (3.0L V6) and CLS 300 added as entry petrol options

CGI direct injection suffers carbon buildup on intake valves

faq.h2: Mercedes Benz CLS Engine Replacement - Frequently Asked Questions

items.question: How much does a Mercedes Benz CLS engine replacement cost in the UK?
items.answer: A rebuilt Mercedes Benz CLS engine typically costs £2,847 to £9,500 supply-only, depending on engine code and variant. The OM642 diesel (CLS 350 CDI) averages £3,200-£5,200, while the M156 AMG 6.2L ranges from £6,500 to £9,500.
keyPoints: CLS 250 CDI (OM651) rebuilt: £2,800-£4,500
CLS 350 petrol (M272) rebuilt: £2,847-£5,200
Additional £450-£900 for supply and fit by AMG-trained technicians
items.cta: Compare Mercedes Benz CLS engine replacement costs for your specific variant

items.question: Is the OM642 diesel engine in the CLS reliable or should I avoid it?
items.answer: The OM642 3.0 V6 diesel is fundamentally robust and capable of 200,000+ miles, but it has a well-documented Achilles heel: oil cooler seal failure that allows coolant to mix with oil. Catch it early and it's a great engine.
keyPoints: Oil cooler seals harden and fail at 70,000-120,000 miles
Turbocharger can be damaged if oil contamination is ignored
CLS 350 CDI is considered the best diesel of C219/C218 generations
Preventative seal replacement costs £650-£950 at a specialist
items.cta: Get quotes for OM642 rebuilt engines for your CLS 350 CDI

items.question: What are the common problems with the M156 engine in the CLS 63 AMG?
items.answer: The M156 6.2L V8 is a masterpiece but has a critical flaw: head bolts on pre-2011 engines can snap, allowing coolant into cylinders. Camshaft adjuster wear and hydraulic tappet noise are also common on higher-mileage cars.
keyPoints: Head bolt failure typically occurs between 40,000-60,000 miles
Symptoms include coolant loss, white smoke, and misfire on cold start
Preventative head bolt upgrade costs £2,200-£3,200 at an AMG specialist
Post-2011 cars have the updated bolt design
items.cta: Compare M156 rebuilt AMG engine prices for your CLS 63 AMG

items.question: How long do Mercedes Benz CLS engines last before needing replacement?
items.answer: A well-maintained CLS diesel (OM642 or OM651) typically reaches 200,000-300,000 miles before major work is needed. Petrol V6 engines (M272/M276) often last 150,000-200,000 miles. The M156 AMG can reach similar figures if head bolts are addressed.
keyPoints: OM642 diesel regularly exceeds 250,000 miles with proper maintenance
OM651 four-cylinder diesel is known for 300,000+ miles on some examples
Most CLS cars average 8,600 miles per year
Only 0.8% of CLS vehicles reach 200,000 miles based on market data
items.cta: Get quotes for a long-life rebuilt Mercedes Benz CLS engine

items.question: Should I replace or repair my Mercedes Benz CLS engine when it fails?
items.answer: For OM642 oil seal failure caught early, repair is excellent value (£650-£950). For M272 timing chain tensioner failure, replacement timing components cost £900-£1,400. For M156 head bolt failure with coolant contamination, full rebuilt engine is usually the smarter option.
keyPoints: Preventative repairs are always better value than reactive ones
If the bottom end is damaged, replacement is the only reliable fix
Specialist rebuilt engines include upgraded components addressing original failure points
On a CLS worth £4,000-£8,000, weigh repair cost against car's overall condition
items.cta: Compare Mercedes Benz CLS engine rebuild vs replacement costs

items.question: Which Mercedes Benz CLS engine is the most reliable overall?
items.answer: The CLS 350 CDI with the OM642 V6 diesel (2010-2014) is widely considered the most reliable CLS engine, combining robust engineering with strong fuel economy. The later OM654 in C257 models also shows excellent reliability but is newer with less long-term data.
keyPoints: CLS 350 CDI (OM642) is the "best diesel of all CLS generations"
CLS 350 (M276 petrol, post-2014) is very reliable with few major issues
CLS 350d (OM654, C257) praised as "one of the most modern diesels ever"
Avoid early M272 petrol engines (pre-2008) with balance shaft issues
items.cta: Find a reliable reconditioned Mercedes Benz CLS engine

items.question: Does the Mercedes Benz CLS 250 CDI (OM651) have timing chain problems?
items.answer: The OM651 2.1L diesel does not have the catastrophic timing chain failures of some competitors, but it does suffer from EGR valve failure, swirl flap mechanism breakdown, and injector seal leaks. Timing chain issues are rare when oil changes are done on schedule.
keyPoints: EGR valve failure causes rough idle and check engine light
Swirl flap failure in intake manifold reduces power significantly
Injector seal leaks create ticking noise and diesel smell
With proper maintenance, OM651 can exceed 300,000 miles
items.cta: Get quotes for OM651 rebuilt engine for your CLS 250 CDI

items.question: Is it worth replacing the engine in my high-mileage Mercedes Benz CLS?
items.answer: If your CLS is otherwise in excellent condition - no rust, good gearbox, well-maintained interior - a rebuilt engine from a specialist is often worthwhile. On a car worth £4,000-£12,000, a £3,000-£5,000 rebuilt engine with 12-month warranty can give you another 100,000+ miles.
keyPoints: Check for rust on subframes and rear arches before committing
Gearbox condition matters - 7G-Tronic and 9G-Tronic are generally robust
A specialist rebuilt engine includes upgraded components to prevent original failures
Compare the cost of replacement against buying another used CLS
items.cta: Check Mercedes Benz CLS engine replacement value for your car

items.question: What is the difference between petrol and diesel Mercedes Benz CLS engines for long-term ownership?
items.answer: Diesels (OM642, OM651) dominate UK CLS sales and offer 45-55 mpg real-world economy, making them cheaper to run for high-mileage drivers. Petrols (M272, M276, M278) are rarer, smoother, and more enjoyable to drive, but return 25-35 mpg and have different failure patterns.
keyPoints: Diesel: higher torque for towing, better motorway cruiser, AdBlue system maintenance required on BlueTEC models
Petrol: smoother power delivery, no DPF or AdBlue issues, lower mileage examples available
OM642 diesel oil seal failures are predictable and preventable
M276 petrol direct injection requires carbon cleaning periodically
items.cta: Compare Mercedes Benz CLS diesel vs petrol engine prices

items.question: Do Mercedes Benz CLS 450 and CLS 53 AMG mild hybrid engines have problems?
items.answer: The M256 3.0L inline-six mild hybrid is generally reliable, but early C257 models (2018-2020) have reported 48V electrical system faults and water pump failures. The combustion engine itself is robust, with most issues being electrical rather than mechanical.
keyPoints: 48V battery module failure affects EQ Boost function
Water pump leaks reported on some early M256 engines
Software updates resolve many electrical gremlins
Full engine replacement is rare - most problems stop at the 48V system
items.cta: Enquire about M256 MHEV engine availability for your CLS 450 or 53 AMG
